I'm not sure about this change. Scaling the images requires both decompressing and recompressing textures, which negatively affects their quality and load times. This will be especially noticeable on mobile, since ETC2 compression is particularly slow. 

Additionally, since the compression libraries are not bundled in export template builds, the textures will remain uncompressed and take up more VRAM.

The engine has a feature similar to this one, implemented in CompressedTexture via the `p_size_limit` parameter in the `load_image_from_file` function. It makes the engine skip the first few mipmap levels and load only the smaller ones. It's currently unused and has a few issues, but #109047 resolves most of them (although it doesn't re-enable their functionality)

- `0.2500000000` (got 1, expected 2)
- `0.1250000000` (got 1, expected 3)
- `0.0000002500` (got 7, expected 8)

I then went to your branch, patched in my extra tests and ran it and got even more failures.

Looking at those three tests, I don't think they failed.

The decimal that is returned (though there isn't any good doc strings on the function) is the place of the first non-zero digit.

e.g., `0.1` returns `1` and `0.001` returns `3`. For some reason my brain thinks of it as "number of zeros after the decimal point, plus 1". I don't know why my brain thinks that way.

If that is what the function is meant to do, then it is working as advertised.

However, I agree that this may not be the expected way the editor inspector works. I suspect that the inspector code is going to need a string based float parser to get the results we expect.

Each of these macros do bind to `ClassDB`. But arguably, they bind more to `Object` because `get_class_static()` is called, which assumes that the caller is an `Object`. 

At the same time, while we use `ClassDB` for registration right now, we (hopefully) won't in the future, when we'll introduce a proper core typing system. Once that happens, we will have to rewrite and move the macros to the typing header anyway.

This is a great effort. Those implicit copies keep haunting us, and we really should be getting rid of them.

Unfortunately, this change is not without risk. Taking a reference instead of making a copy makes the caller vulnerable to changes during execution of the code that uses these variables. If the underlying data is modified, this could lead to crashes, data corruption, or subtle bugs.
Since this change is motivated and informed by a syntactical change (making the constructors `explicit`), rather than a profiler, I think there's too much risk of regression to make this a blanket change. I might be swayed if you go through all of the code, and make absolutely sure there is no risk of mutation regressions, but I'm not sure if this is worth it (since we don't know if any of this is in performance critical code).

If I were to make this change, I would simply make the call explicit syntactically (via e.g. `List(other_list)`), to keep the syntactic change agnostic to possible changes in behavior. If the code pops up in a profiler in the future, it can more easily be fixed, because the copy is explicit and therefore an obvious target for optimization.

- `StringName(const char *)` can be faster (about 60%) than `StringName(const String &)` if no `String` exists yet, but a `StringName` entry does exist. This can be relevant depending on the application.

The `stop` method currently has two meanings: pause and abort. If we want to keep the queue, we should probably only keep it with the pause behavior. However, to maintain compatibility, it would be safer to add an optional argument like `keep_queue` to `stop()` with `false` as default (then we can remove `breaks compat` label). When interrupting to play another animation while queue is playing, it goes without saying that you must ensure the previous queue is cleared (as if remain queue in this case would constitute a change in behavior many old projects and should be avoided). Additionally, using a queue means crossfades will occur. Therefore, you must also test whether the stop processing during crossfades is performed correctly. Moreover, note that crossfades can occur simultaneously with three or more animations for example the case `Anim1: length = 3.0s fade = 2.0s, Anim2: length = 1.0s fade = 0.5s, Anim3: length = 2.0s fade = 0.5s`. You can set the crossfade from the default blend time or edit transition. > I also don't know why it was being cleared in the first place, if someone knows, please let me know! This behavior existed even before I touched the animation code. My guess is that it's difficult to preserve the state during crossfades, and it's likely to prevent breaking the behavior when the queue is modified while paused during a crossfade.

Reserving and passing explicit counts makes the code more complicated. Please focus on the cases where we know it will have an impact, and show that the performance is improved by profiling or benchmarking before and after the change.

You can basically remove any call to resize that is guaranteed to be less than 13 since those cases will be the same performance or slightly slower with the added call to resize. 

This highlights why it is important to profile. If you checked these "optimizations" with a profiler you would have seen that the added code doesn't make a difference. 

Please read our optimization guidelines https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/guidelines/optimization.html which explain a bit more the thought process you should engage in when deciding to optimize code. Importantly, you should only spend time optimizing code that actually impacts the run time performance of games. Its not really a good use of time to add code that saves a few microseconds in a method that is only called once during some debug method. In fact, its a net negative since it just makes the code more complex for no tangible benefit

Hi, thanks for submitting a fix! 

Have you checked that this solves the problem in the intended way? 
Reasoning about the code, I would expect `CRASH_NOW_MSG` to try to print, causing a new `ScriptBacktrace` object to be instantiated, entering `ObjectDB::add_instance`... which runs into the same slot count problem, causing recursive loop and eventually, a stack overflow.

Generally, printing to the standard console won't ever be perfectly secure because even if we don't, custom loggers could always try to create new objects.

I can think of 3 solutions that might be better (not sure which would be best):
1) Crash _before_ we run out of slots. But that would reduce our total slot counts by 1, so I'm not sure this would be a well liked solution.
2) Crash without printing a message
3) Print using a lower-level API instead, guaranteeing that no `Object` will be created. The message won't reach the console, but at least it will reach the logs

Thanks! Optimizing `sprintf` sounds like a worthy goal to me. However, I have some reservations:

We've had some lengthy discussions over `StringBuilder` a year or so ago, and my current understanding is that it might not be as useful for optimization purposes as previously believed, especially when compared to `StringBuffer`. However, I have not pursued this investigation yet.
I suspect the main bottleneck to be solved here is `String`'s CoW checks when adding new chars individually. Using `StringBuilder` circumvents that, but so would `StringBuffer`. I'd like to see a performance comparison between the two in this context before I'd feel comfortable giving this an approval.

Outside the approach taken in the PR, I'd like to ask you to separate the `StringBuilder` changes from the `String` changes. I've previously attempted to optimize `StringBuilder` in a similar way, but found that there was no difference to the current implementation. So whether the re-write is an improvement should be tested separately.
Since this has a run-time performance impact, it needs to be tested to ensure that there isn't a performance regression.

I must **heavily** note that I am not part of the Input team, nor I have much agency to get this PR in a stable state and potentially accepted. So take the following with a grain of salt.

From what I understand, this PR does not touch the Input Map panel much. It's a missed opportunity though, as it means that different input events assigned to different players are hard to discern.

The current system works well, but seemingly falls apart when multiple players are using the same device. It somewhat defeats its purpose, as code would still be required to work around it, but it's also potentially inevitable and that I can understand.

I'm personally debating whether it's better to have the flexibility of a bitmask, at the cost of essentially limiting the "players" to `32`/`64` (which is a ridiculous amount anyway) and verbosity (having to rely on constants instead of simpler numbers).
I am inclined to favour a bitmask approach, mostly because it's more distinct. To explain, the current "individual player" approach is almost no different than creating multiple similar actions for each player (either [programmatically](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/classes/class_inputmap.html#class-inputmap-method-add-action) or manually), then querying the action with some code (for example `is_action("jump_" + str(player_number))`. That is to say, it already _can_ be done, and this PR as is currently only serves as a quality-of-life addition.
While a bitmask would make the system more customizable, it's also quite... useless? I can hardly think of any reason to query more than one player at once. It's either "all players" or "one player". At least for the purposes of this PR, because the bitmask is actually necessary in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/102412 .

I don't think this is an improvement.
Calls are not that expensive in most contexts. In this case, it is not expensive because it will be inlined. Therefore, we do not need to "protect" ourselves against making the call.

Besides, one of the first things `CowData` does is check `p_size == size()`. If you're doing it here too, that's simply more work that needs to be done by the CPU, without helping in a practical way.

What's more important though is what (I think) Clay is alluding to — what we should do to improve performance of the engine is to profile it, identify bottlenecks, and then show improvements through systematic tests. Performance PRs must therefore _always_ come with a performance test.

Hmm... I guess this logic mirrors the "add child" logic of keeping the cache when possible? So I can see where you're coming from.

But, I wonder how common the case is of removing children from the back of a node. I imagine it's less common than adding nodes to the back.
If removing children from the back isn't very common, or usually done in a loop (removing many at once), this wouldn't be much of an improvement. So if we want to motivate merging this, I'd like to see:

- A benchmark of how much of an improvement is expected in the 'happy case' (i.e. removing from the back)
- Some profile result of a game/project where removing children appears or is a bottleneck.

If we don't find a benchmark result that motivates this, we can keep the PR up in draft mode, and merge it if it does become relevant.

Makes sense in principle to have `String` conversions that don't involve `Variant`.

I'm a bit torn on adding normal constructors to `String`. Currently, it's not possible to e.g. convert from `double` to `String` without calling `num_real` explicitly. 
On the one hand, being able to convert anything to `String` is convenient and expected. Like the `str` function in `Python`, you can just expect anything to describe itself conveniently.
On the other hand, it's not 100% unambiguous what a conversion from a primitive to `String` means (bool and `nullptr_t` especially). For example in Rust, this is solved not by conversion operators, but by implementing traits `Debug` and `Display`, which are a bit more explicit about what kind of `String` conversion is expected. 

So in the end, I'm not sure we want to add non-contextual `String` conversion by default. Maybe something for another core meeting?

## Pull Request Overview This PR ensures consistent argument ordering when using both `bind` and `unbind` operations on signal connections. The key change updates the parsing logic to check for `CallableCustomUnbind` before `CallableCustomBind`, reflecting the correct chaining order: `callable.bind().unbind()` results in a structure where unbind wraps bind. - Updated the order of checking callable custom types to match the actual structure created by chaining bind and unbind operations - Moved unbinds display after binds in the connections dock UI for clearer presentation - Removed unused `bind_controls` vector from ConnectDialog - Fixed formatting of `binds=` attribute in resource text format (removed extra space) - Adjusted blank line placement for better code organization ### Reviewed Changes Copilot reviewed 4 out of 5 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | scene/resources/resource_format_text.cpp | Fixed formatting by removing extra space before `binds=` | | scene/resources/packed_scene.cpp | Reordered callable unwrapping to check unbind before bind, matching actual structure | | editor/scene/connections_dialog.h | Applied same unwrapping order fix and removed unused `bind_controls` field | | editor/scene/connections_dialog.cpp | Removed unused `bind_controls` population and reordered unbinds display after binds | | core/variant/callable.cpp | Added missing includes for List and Vector templates |

Unfortunately, we cannot simply reuse the old macro; at least, not without further adjustments. While this correctly binds, documentation is mangled because a class/namespace is now prefixed. For instance, even without changing an existing enum like `MouseMode` to a scoped equivalent, prefixing the class will still break that output:
```cpp
	BIND_ENUM_CONSTANT(MouseMode::MOUSE_MODE_VISIBLE);
	BIND_ENUM_CONSTANT(MouseMode::MOUSE_MODE_HIDDEN);
	BIND_ENUM_CONSTANT(MouseMode::MOUSE_MODE_CAPTURED);
	BIND_ENUM_CONSTANT(MouseMode::MOUSE_MODE_CONFINED);
	BIND_ENUM_CONSTANT(MouseMode::MOUSE_MODE_CONFINED_HIDDEN);
	BIND_ENUM_CONSTANT(MouseMode::MOUSE_MODE_MAX);
```
```diff
 	<constants>
-		<constant name="MOUSE_MODE_VISIBLE" value="0" enum="MouseMode">
-			Makes the mouse cursor visible if it is hidden.
+		<constant name="MouseMode::MOUSE_MODE_VISIBLE" value="0" enum="MouseMode">
 		</constant>
-		<constant name="MOUSE_MODE_HIDDEN" value="1" enum="MouseMode">
-			Makes the mouse cursor hidden if it is visible.
+		<constant name="MouseMode::MOUSE_MODE_HIDDEN" value="1" enum="MouseMode">
 		</constant>
-		<constant name="MOUSE_MODE_CAPTURED" value="2" enum="MouseMode">
-			Captures the mouse. The mouse will be hidden and its position locked at the center of the window manager's window.
-			[b]Note:[/b] If you want to process the mouse's movement in this mode, you need to use [member InputEventMouseMotion.relative].
+		<constant name="MouseMode::MOUSE_MODE_CAPTURED" value="2" enum="MouseMode">
 		</constant>
-		<constant name="MOUSE_MODE_CONFINED" value="3" enum="MouseMode">
-			Confines the mouse cursor to the game window, and make it visible.
+		<constant name="MouseMode::MOUSE_MODE_CONFINED" value="3" enum="MouseMode">
 		</constant>
-		<constant name="MOUSE_MODE_CONFINED_HIDDEN" value="4" enum="MouseMode">
-			Confines the mouse cursor to the game window, and make it hidden.
+		<constant name="MouseMode::MOUSE_MODE_CONFINED_HIDDEN" value="4" enum="MouseMode">
 		</constant>
-		<constant name="MOUSE_MODE_MAX" value="5" enum="MouseMode">
-			Max value of the [enum MouseMode].
+		<constant name="MouseMode::MOUSE_MODE_MAX" value="5" enum="MouseMode">
 		</constant>
```
---
We *do* already have macros for handling enum classes, but those are currently isolated to `core_constants.cpp`. Specifically: `BIND_CORE_ENUM_CLASS_CONSTANT` and its variants.
```cpp
#define BIND_CORE_ENUM_CLASS_CONSTANT(m_enum, m_prefix, m_member)                                                  \
	{                                                                                                              \
		StringName enum_name = __constant_get_enum_name(m_enum::m_member);                                         \
		_global_constants.push_back(_CoreConstant(enum_name, #m_prefix "_" #m_member, (int64_t)m_enum::m_member)); \
		_global_constants_map[#m_prefix "_" #m_member] = _global_constants.size() - 1;                             \
		_global_enums[enum_name].push_back((_global_constants.ptr())[_global_constants.size() - 1]);               \
	}
```

They do more than simply casting the value and trimming the prefix though. This is because scoped enums take advantage of the fact that their class scope is required, and thus their contents have much shorter names. So if `MouseMode` was truly a scoped enum, it'd necessitate the following change:
```cpp
	enum class MouseMode {
		VISIBLE,
		HIDDEN,
		CAPTURED,
		CONFINED,
		CONFINED_HIDDEN,
		MAX,
	};
```
Otherwise declared variables would be comically verbose. In the most extreme case, failing to do so would result in `MouseMode::MOUSE_MODE_CONFINED_HIDDEN`.

This is a totally acceptable change, though! That's because the nature of using a scoped enum means that a ton of code will need to be touched anyway to account for explicit conversions and mandatory prefixes. In that same pass, there's no reason *not* to adjust the value names as well.

If this was a core enum type, we'd pass it to the macro which handles all this noise automatically:
```cpp
	BIND_CORE_ENUM_CLASS_CONSTANT(MouseMode, MOUSE_MODE, VISIBLE);
	BIND_CORE_ENUM_CLASS_CONSTANT(MouseMode, MOUSE_MODE, HIDDEN);
	BIND_CORE_ENUM_CLASS_CONSTANT(MouseMode, MOUSE_MODE, CAPTURED);
	BIND_CORE_ENUM_CLASS_CONSTANT(MouseMode, MOUSE_MODE, CONFINED);
	BIND_CORE_ENUM_CLASS_CONSTANT(MouseMode, MOUSE_MODE, CONFINED_HIDDEN);
	BIND_CORE_ENUM_CLASS_CONSTANT(MouseMode, MOUSE_MODE, MAX);
```
Everything outside of core has no such luxury. As such, I'd rather not integrate this change, as it'd hide a footgun of our own design. We absolutely *should* be able to support enum class binds out-of-the-box, but it needs to be accompanied with proper support for documentation

The essence of the issue mentioned in https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/12382 and https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/6709 is that manual assignment when bone estimation fails is cumbersome.

I'm skeptical about this PR's approach if it want to address the issue, because it's too narrow in scope and lacks reusability; Specifically, it is necessary to precisely specify the symbols for distinguishing left and right, and it cannot be applied to spine bones.

Therefore, I suggest implementing a process where manual assignment is followed by manually executing bone estimation based on those assignments. That approach would allows for the reuse of the existing bone estimation method that utilizes RegEx for left/right determination which has been already included.

In my opinion, this approach is one of the acceptable options for solving linked issues, but probably not the most conservative one. My main concern is that it changes behavior in a way that could be considered a breaking change. So we should add the label and mention this change in the migration guide, on the release page, etc.

At the same time, this approach has the advantage of minimal simplicity. Alternative solutions would likely lead to unnecessary complexity and configurability.

Also, as far as I understand, this actually doesn't violate the JSON specification. We can represent `Number` as either `int` or `float`. And the same number can have multiple representations (like `1` and `1.0`). But we're not required to use the shortest notation when encoding, and we're not prohibited from exploiting the notation differences to choose the target type when decoding (even if it can be unreliable). For example, PHP also relies on the fractional part to choose the target type:

```php
<?php

var_dump(json_encode(1)); // string(1) "1"
var_dump(json_encode(1.0)); // string(1) "1"
var_dump(json_encode(1.0, JSON_PRESERVE_ZERO_FRACTION)); // string(3) "1.0"
var_dump(json_encode(1.1)); // string(3) "1.1"

var_dump(json_decode("1")); // int(1)
var_dump(json_decode("1.0")); // float(1)
var_dump(json_decode("1.1")); // float(1.1)
```
Makes sense, but do we want a separate property? This is what most people would expect from `fit_to_longest_item`.

I think this is the wrong way to fix it.

The ghosting issue with undo fundamentally doesn't depend on the AnimationPlayerEditor. If you remove the AnimationPlayer in your MRP, and directly swap the Sprite2D texture, and delete previous texture and performing undo, ghosting should still appear there.

Therefore, handling undoredo solely within the AnimationPlayerEditor is at least misguided. If you want to eliminate ghosting, a fundamental implementation change is likely required.

As pointed out in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/114637#issuecomment-3717364226, I think adding some cleanup to AnimationPlayerEditor when closing Godot is the right approach, but this PR is currently doing the wrong thing.

The biggest considerations when merging PRs like this are:
1. What tangible benefit does this provide users? A 13% performance improvement is nice, but a 13% improvement on a function that is called only a handful of times doesn't really do anything. More directly, how often is this called in a frame and how many microseconds does this save in a typical frame?
2. The risk of bugs. Any change to the codebase risks introducing bugs. From looking at the code alone I can't tell how the behaviour differs, or even if it differs. But experience tells me that many safe-looking PRs can introduce bugs when edge-cases aren't properly considered. 

Basically, the performance benefit in actual games needs to justify the risk we take in merging this. Saving 25,000 microseconds is really nice, but nobody is calling this function 300 million times in a loop in their game. So what is the actual benefit for real games?

As a serious issue, results become unstable when there are three or more holes. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4b49b92d-b03c-4c44-8407-278d8b7cb853 Based on testing, the result is stable with two holes. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8f1fe8bc-aae9-4e0a-9580-eeadab058f6e [kusudama_test.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25640319/kusudama_test.zip) Therefore, if we implement this in 4.7, we should consider statically limiting the number of holes to two. If we statically limit holes to two, two structures come to mind. --- One is the same as the current implementation and just having two holes internally: ```cpp struct HoleSetting { Vector3 direction; real_t radius; } HoleSetting begin_hole; HoleSetting end_hole; ``` --- The other approach is to eliminate the Vector specification and instead treat it as a arc-shaped area spreading in the ±Z directions, with the offset delegated to JointLimitation: ```cpp real_t arc_angle; // 0 to 360deg. How to spread start and end to +Z and -Z. for example, 180deg means start hole dir is Vector3(0, 0, -1) and end hole dir is Vector3(0, 0, 1). real_t start_radius; // -Z real_t end_radius; // +Z ``` Offset is set by: The reason it expands in the Z direction is because HumanoidProfile defines X rotation as a Bend. In other words, specifying individual vectors makes sense when there are three or more holes. However, when there are only two holes, specifying individual vectors is redundant with the rotation offset parameter. This means the hole direction can be automatically generated from the arc parameter without necessarily specifying it individually. --- Personally, I think the latter approach makes the role of the offset clearer and allows for more intuitive range manipulation. If we don't plan to abandon implementing the three holes in the future (after 4.7), I think providing the two static hole approach as a class called JointLimitationArc is a somewhat good compromise. How do you think?

The core problem here is that `material_set_shader` is not following a read-only constraint on shader and modifying it instead. Pushing the call to the command queue introduces a race between the mesh loading and the server thread where it'll fail to precompile pipelines in time if the command queue hasn't gotten to it yet.

The proper fix would be to fix `material_set_shader` instead. As it stands, the PR introduces a potential performance/stutter regression that will be very difficult to detect in the future as to why it happens.

The issue is more complex than the min GPU, when baking shaders.

Unfortunately, when shaders are queried in the renderer, it asks the **host** platform for supported features, such as `RD::SUPPORTS_IMAGE_ATOMIC_32_BIT` (the primary culprit of the baking issues). On the host platform, this returns true. When baking the shader, the minimum GPU and Metal versions are passed to SPIRV-Cross, based on the MetalDeviceProfile. By default, the versions we pass do not support atomics, so it generates unsupported shader code, as SPIRV-Cross tries to emulate atomics. 

> [!IMPORTANT]
>
> If the user specified a target OS of iOS 17 (Metal 3.1) for iOS, the baked shaders should work. For earlier OS versions, Godot should fall back to compiling the code.

When paired with https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/97646, that PR's **Write Depth** property will make heightmaps cast contact shadows:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fa139753-3bd9-460d-aef0-45d4764ac336

Some feedback:

- The maximum length a contact shadow can cover depends on viewport resolution (e.g. changing **Scaling 3D > Scale** will affect this length). Higher resolutions reduce the contact shadow's maximum length. This isn't ideal for reasons similar to [#54518](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/54518), but it ensures performance figures don't vary too much across resolutions.

- In terms of API design, I think the property in DirectionalLight3D should be called **Allow Contact Shadows**, as it does not enable contact shadows by itself. Instead, you need to enable contact shadows in the Environment resource.

- Changing **Max Steps** appears to have no effect (visual or performance).

I did a test on the TPS demo where I replaced its soundtrack (112 Kb/s Vorbis, 3 minutes of audio, 2.6 MB total) with Opus-encoded versions (64 Kb/s Opus). This provides a 1.3 MB size reduction, which stays roughly intact in the exported PCK size:

- `master` with Vorbis soundtrack: 582,804 bytes
- This PR with Opus soundtrack: 581,692 bytes (-1,112 KB)

The same goes in an exported ZIP data pack (therefore with compression):

- `master` with Vorbis soundtrack: 252,776 bytes
- This PR with Opus soundtrack: 251,772 bytes (-1,004 KB)

Tip: Use this shell script to mass-convert Vorbis to Opus (run at project root):

```bash
shopt -s globstar nullglob
for audio in **/*.ogg; do
  ffmpeg -hide_banner -i $audio -b:a 64k ${audio//.ogg/.opus} -y
done
```

Remember to assign the .opus files in AudioStreamPlayer nodes, and move the .ogg originals outside the project folder afterwards so that they're not included in the exported PCK.

For best results, you should encode Opus audio from a lossless source (WAV or FLAC), as encoding from a lossy codec to another will make the loss of quality more significant. 64 Kb/s Opus should be equivalent to 128 Kb/s Vorbis when both are encoded from a lossless source, but this may not hold true when re-encoding lossy Vorbis audio.

I see this PR was based on #47114, which was from 2021, so this PR missed the modern patterns :
- no overrides such as `tag_used_streams`, `set_parameter`, `get_parameter`, `set_is_sample`, `get_is_sample`, `get_sample_playback`, `set_sample_playback`
- `data` using `void*` instead of the container like `TightLocalVector`
- Still initialize the values in the constructor instead of initializing as default values
- properties like `length` and `loop_offset` are still using `float`. But sibling implementations like Vorbis use `double` (MP3 is using `float` too)

Compared with [my own implementation from almost a year ago (outdated branch, not based on #47114)](https://github.com/gongpha/godot/tree/opus), I am skeptical about `_mix_internal`'s infinite loop handling. But I think I would leave it there if there is no significant issue or crash

I feel that what this PR is trying to achieve does not make much sense. Although SkeletonModificationStack2D is experimental, to be honest making it a Resource was a design failure as pointed out in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/117090#issuecomment-4009741726. That is the reason why SkeletonModificationStack3D was removed at the launch of 4.0 and SkeletonModifier3D was added instead.

As a class that uses similar resources, there is AnimationBlendTree, which holds the current state as a parameter dictionary in instances of AnimationTree node. If you want to correctly instantiate SkeletonModification2D as a shared Resource, you would need to have its parent, Skeleton2D node, hold something like a dictionary to store things such as the transform information from the previous frame for IK, for example `skeleton_2d->store_mod_parameter(*self, SNAME("prev_transform"), t2d);`. Otherwise, if a ModificationStack needs to retain prev_rot, a conflict may occur when sharing a reference. For instance, if SkeletonA (prev_rot: 0.5) and SkeletonB (prev_rot: 1.0) share the same ModificationStack and are processed sequentially, SkeletonA's prev_rot will be incorrectly overwritten by 1.0 in the ModificationStack.

However, as also mentioned in https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/10247, I believe that SkeletonModification2D should ultimately be deprecated and migrated to SkeletonModifier2D.

To solve the problem in #117090, the correct path is to proceed with the implementation of SkeletonModifier2D, but that will likely take time. While this PR hides the errors, it should not change the fact that they are not working correctly. I think the only thing we can do here now is to issue a warning that Skeleton2D, which holds a SkeletonModification2D, should be deep-copied and made unique when instantiated.

Some feedback:

- `FWPLUS` in warnings and enums shouldn't be abbreviated. Instead, we should write it in its full form `FORWARD_PLUS`, like we do in the renderer name.

- To disable motion vectors on an object, you need to write a very small but *non-zero* value (e.g. `vec2(0.0000001)`). If you write `vec2(0.0)`, the default motion vectors based on camera motion will apply. This isn't very intuitive, so maybe there should be `render_mode motion_vector_disabled` for this.
  - See also https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/77523.

- Whenever motion vectors are overridden, camera motion no longer influences motion vectors. Is there a way to keep both? This is what we need for scrolling texture UVs, where we want the constant motion of the scrolling texture to affect motion, but preserve automatic motion vectors from camera motion.
  - Setting `MOTION_VECTOR` to a very high value makes the scrolling texture sharp as if TAA was disabled, but it makes anything in front jitter heavily, so it's not a correct solution.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/57bb9f1d-965d-4d05-8f43-1a94a8d25cea

- Motion vectors seem dependent on the viewport aspect ratio (like normalized UV coordinates), unlike the automatic default motion vector. You need to account for this when writing your shader. Is this how it works in other engines? If not, I can see this being a pitfall for supporting multiple aspect ratios.

Tested locally, it works as expected. **Testing project:** [test_ssil.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26635077/test_ssil.zip) ### Before #### Normal https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5ce289cc-bfcb-438c-8ff4-408d5c594807 #### Ultra https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c4b682b4-2f74-4629-96f2-ac26c42bc3c7 ### After #### Normal https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e3a5c970-a545-4653-9c20-d118484caea2 #### Ultra https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fded556d-e576-4535-9513-6085ec0009bb Some feedback: - With this PR, the SSIL range becomes resolution-dependent (old SSIL did not have this issue). At higher resolutions, SSIL doesn't go as far: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fd976fdb-a19d-4229-9575-3e4ceaa15f03 This also affects toggling the **SSIL > Half Size** project setting. This makes adjustments more difficult, as the appearance effectively varies according to window size and the 3D resolution scale factor. If possible, look into adjusting the SSIL range automatically relative to a base viewport resolution (e.g. 1080 pixels tall). If you manually double the range when the viewport resolution is doubled on each axis, you get roughly the same SSIL appearance: Half resolution | Full resolution | Full resolution, doubled range -|-|- | | - The default quality leads to rather noisy visuals, especially when strong emissives are involved. To improve quality without affecting performance too much, I suggest reintroducing the noise, but only jitter it every frame when TAA or FSR2 is enabled. Keep the noise static otherwise, which should make it less noticeable. - We do something similar for shadow maps already: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/61834 - Look into using interleaved gradient noise: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/9495deaf7532313ff7154d8a06aa6fc100807532/servers/rendering/renderer_rd/shaders/environment/sky.glsl#L142-L147 TAA jitter can be performed by adding a offset every frame, with `taa_frame_count` being equal to `float(RSG::rasterizer->get_frame_number() % p_jitter_phase_count)`: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/9495deaf7532313ff7154d8a06aa6fc100807532/servers/rendering/renderer_rd/shaders/scene_forward_lights_inc.glsl#L305 - Backface rejection is now off by default, which likely explains why visuals can change so much in some scenes. It also means indirect lighting can be less temporally stable, especially when faces that are nearly parallel to the camera slowly become visible like the ceiling here: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d263505a-88ef-4487-b668-e46a93cb2574

Looks good in terms of code, though I think the UV scale should be slightly adjusted as currently `CSGCylinder` and `CSGTorus` UVs are way more dense than those of other shapes.
Here's what I believe they should look like:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b868db83-2cfe-47c0-be9f-843ab6801d1f)

Notably, `CSGCylinder` UVs should be scaled 2 times around the cylinder instead of 4, and they should be simply calculated with `i` instead of `inverse_i`, so the textures will match with the top/bottom ones respectively (like with `CSGCube`).

As for `CSGTorus`, `float inci_uv = 4.0 / ring_sides;` should be `float inci_uv = 2.0 / ring_sides;`.

This is just a suggestion though, this PR already makes them look way better than their current state.

This feels like fake bindless. I don't think the "big number" approach is the way to go about it. This also has the problem of leaving descriptors uninitialized, which is illegal without the `VK_DESCRIPTOR_BINDING_PARTIALLY_BOUND_BIT` flag.

For now, I think it would make more sense if you manually specified the upper array bounds in your GLSL, and filled the unused space with a "default" buffer in your GDScript code. For that, we do need the support for multiple storage buffers from here.

Actually, I noticed a potential issue. This can still happen with the initial code you submitted.

Imagine the following scenario:
- The MSAA framebuffer gets created. The intermediate texture gets attached to it in the Android path.
- At some point in the next frame, "use_internal_buffer" becomes false.
- The intermediate texture gets freed. The MSAA framebuffer is now attached to stale, freed textures, because it thinks there is no need to reconfigure it.

This scenario cannot happen in upstream because the MSAA framebuffer gets recreated, or is configured to be ignored, each time the function is called.
Makes sense to me.

While i understand the notion, I'm not sure we can merge this change.

Currently we just treat freed objects as `nil` (at least for `==`). With this change, we would treat freed objects as "tombstones with an ID". This is a major semantic change that might lead to problems all over people's projects.

I think we'll have to solve https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/118422 differently to avoid regressions.
